# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Best thriller movie you have watched in your life. THE BEST
I'M REQUESTING

**Score:** 92
**Body:** I'm looking for the absolute best thriller movie you've ever watched—the one that had you completely hooked from beginning to end. I love intense suspense, unpredictable plot twists, psychological mind games, and edge-of-your-seat tension. Any language is fine as long as it's genuinely amazing. What's your #1 recommendation and why?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Prisoners (2013) never loosens its grip, just two and a half hours of pure mounting dread with Hugh Jackman at his absolute best.
2. Arlington road
3. Not sure anything can beat Seven for me.
4. The Fugitive 
5. Silence of the Lambs. Shattered the genre. Se7en showed its power and Nocturnal Animals for showing a rare beauty in the genre. Melancholia shares all the above!  
6. Calibre 2018. High tension. Excellent acting. Good plot. 
7. The Game
8. Rear Window
9. Seven, Silence of the Lambs, The Sixth Sense, Shutter Island (there is a trend here), Taken
10. North by Northwest

## 2. Movies about lonely men

**Score:** 41
**Body:** I need them for research. I am asking for movies when we see:

Men, who is lonely ( no loving family, no GF/BF/whatever, zero to few friends), but without Redpill content in it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Her
2. Lars & the Real Girl
3. The Lonely Guy (1984)  
Stranger than Fiction (2006)
4. Taxi Driver
5. Perfect Days by Wim Wenders 
6. The Station Agent
7. Paris, Texas
8. Love Liza (2002)

About Schmidt (2002)

Broken Flowers (2005)

Lars and the Real Girl (2007)

The Banshees of Inisherin (2022)
9. Planes, Trains, and Automobiles
10. Midnight Cowboy 

## 3. Time Travel Films

**Score:** 43
**Body:** Any suggestions for movies that feature time travelling/repeating days, I’m not looking for something to confusing, just sort of a fun film to watch. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Palm Springs (2020)
2. Edge of tomorrow / Live. Die. Repeat (same movie but two titles, for some reason)
3. About Time. Think it’s an underrated time travel movie.
4. Kate & Leopold 
5. If you’re open to TV series rather than just movies, I have to recommend **Dark** and **1899** (from the same creators).

**Dark** is genuinely one of the best shows I’ve ever seen. It’s one of the highest-rated series on IMDb for a reason. It handles time travel in a way that’s incredibly well thought out, but I won’t spoil anything. Just make sure you watch it in German with subtitles!

If you’re after something a bit more relaxed and easier to binge, **1899** is a great choice. It’s a shorter series (one season) and easier to just sit back and enjoy, while still having that mysterious vibe.

Honestly, *Dark* is the one show I wish I could erase from my memory just so I could experience it for the first time again.
6. Time Bandits 
7. The Time Traveler's Wife
8. Butterfly Effect
9. Somewhere in Time - Christopher Reeve and Jane Seymour…
10. About Time. It’s not the usual time travel film and is more of a romcom with time travel, but is still brilliant.

## 4. Movies that will make me experience USA in 90s/ early 2000s

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I feel pretty nostalgic for USA in 90s even tho i was not even born at the time. I don’t even know how to explain it better, hope you guys understand and thank you in advance 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clueless (1995)
2. Can’t Hardly Wait.

10 Things I Hate About You

Varsity Blues
3. Kids (1995)
4. Singles (1992)


5. If you are looking for like the Millennial childhood, pre-teen, and teenage experience that is of that pre-11 era when were hopeful about our future, I suggest:

Empire Records

10 Things I Hate About You (which is an adaptation of Shakespeare's Taming of the Shrew)

Clueless (which is an adaptation of Jane Austen's Emma)

High Fidelity

You've Got Mail

Can't Hardly Wait

Office Space 

The Devil Wears Prada (this was post- 9/11, but pre-Great Recession so there was still hope)
6. There's a movie with Seth Green as a roller blader with his cousin in Cincinnati, that's what you're looking for. Also, The Mighty Ducks
7. Go!
  - Peak ball knowledge, love that movie
8. Clerks
9. Clerks/Mallrats

Kids

Empire Records

Hackers

Encinoman/Son in Law

House Party

The Craft

Boyz N tha Hood

## 5. Movies that revolve around a house

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Yesterday I finally got around to watching Sentimental Value, and the family home is almost like a character in and of itself. (The way it is described in the narration, how it visibly changes throughout each era/with new residents, how it carries the family history of the characters.)

Now, I'm looking for some suggestions for movies that center a house. Think houses that are haunted by the stories of all who've lived there before, houses that are like the one constant in a movie while the characters only pass through, etc. Any genre is welcome.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Money Pit
2. Life as a house

Evil dead 2

Amityville horror 
3. Encanto

The Others 

Hausu

Edit: a book that fits your genre perfectly is Hotel World by Ali Smith 
4. Not a movie. But The Haunting of Hill House and Bly Manor. 
5. House of Sand and Fog (2003)
6. The Others 
7. Crimson Peak
8. Here
9. The Zone of Interest (2023)

With its manicured garden and spacious interior, the three-story villa was once described as “paradise” by the mother who raised her five children there. And much was done to preserve the household’s tranquility, given its immediate neighbor: the largest and most notorious Nazi concentration camp, Auschwitz.

Theirs was a joyful life. The children played with turtles, cats, rode horses and swam in the nearby river. Meanwhile, the concentration camp’s chimneys spewed smoke as other families were pushed into the gas chambers.

The house and its unique location formed a fundamental part of the Oscar-winning movie "The Zone of Interest".
10. Monster House 2006

## 2. What is the most ridiculous, over the top movie scene that you absolutely love?

**Score:** 1193
**Body:** What’s that one movie sequence that is so OTP and excessive in its craziness, that you can’t help but smirk everytime?

For me it’s the iconic gun leg scene in Planet Terror (2007).

This is scene is something else. Just chaotic and absolute insanity, and I love every second of it!

What’s that movie scene for you?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

2. The whole Hardcore Henry. Or Crank

Just amazing…
3. Twister

There’s an F5 tornado headed at them, they run in a rickety ass hundred-year-old barn.

So they’re safe.

Then they noticed that there are a bunch of knives and blades and sharp instruments hanging up in the hundred year-old rickety barn.

Which makes it unsafe, so now they have to find somewhere else to shelter.

When he's fighting all those dudes in the room then kicks over the oil drum to make the floor slippy, the breaks the clip-in pedals off a bicycle so that he stays grippy while he roundhouse kicks guys in the face and doesn't slip out of the toe holds. Blew my mind as a teenager and I don't even care, It's still fucking awesome

## 3. What Are The Most Preposterous Movie Accents? (John Malkovich as Teddy KGB in Rounders)

**Score:** 652
**Body:** The amount of joy I get from hearing John Malkovich saying *“Pyay That Myan His Myoney”* as Teddy KGB in Rounders has me thinking of all the hilarious movie accents over time.

It can be a purposefully cartoonish, over-the-top actor having fun like Malkovich… an earnest, failed attempt at authenticity like Leo and Cameron Diaz’s wavering Irish accents in Gangs of New York… Don Cheadle’s debatably awful cockney accent in the Ocean’s movies (is it the *character* doing the awful accent?)… or just hilarious “I Give Up” halfway through the film á la Kevin Costner in Robin Hood Prince of Thieves?

Give me your best and worst ridiculous movie accents.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|Vhtcpbuuix9XW)
2. I'm French. Why do think I have this *outrageous* accent!
3. Keanu Reeves in Dracula.

https://preview.redd.it/lcrpy97ql1ah1.jpeg?width=1200&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=949a89ae07547e174e5daf734a313400df7aafa6
4. Don Cheadle Oceans 11, why?
  - I remember there being a theory that the accent is purposefully bad because his character is a con artist who is potentially faking his entire personality.
5. ![gif](giphy|HNQVf0ik57nHy)  
“Ah sayd put the bunnah back in the box!”
6. ![gif](giphy|RMvkZob2sBX5HL9W66)
  - To be fair, his dancing was pretty great in that movie iirc
7. Al Pacino’s Cuban accent was horrendous in Scarface. 
8. Kevin costner’s accent throughout the Robin Hood movie he did. It was amazing how by the end of the film, he wasn’t even trying anymore lol

**Score:** 596

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

The way they all act friendly, hospitable & stupid to make Jake feel comfortable in order to disarm him is well written and acted.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Perfect Days - A meditative movie

**Score:** 129
**Body:** Watched this Japanese movie - Perfect days. The movie felt so deeply touching. The context of the movie goes something like this: Hirayama feels content with his life as a toilet cleaner in Tokyo. Outside his structured routine, he cherishes music on cassette tapes, reads books and takes photos. Through unexpected encounters, he reflects on finding beauty in the world.

The movie makes us feel how in our own lives whatever work we do, in simple sense we can find contentment. It doesn't have to be flashy but yeah there are many worlds within this world. There are moments of sorrow, happiness, stillness, just a joy in playing an old cassette and observing the world in front of us.

And yet moving on living imperfectly in an imperfect world with calmness and joy.

A beautiful cinema!

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love, love, loved it. When his sister shows up, it completely destroys our initial understanding of Hirayama's situation. He clearly didn't have to live the way he did. And the very final shot of him in the car suggest he isn't quite as content as we are led to believe.
  - I feel like he is very grateful for the life he has, and has actively chosen to live this reclusive life, but he also knows he has paid a price for this: company, family. He misses this human contact, and he compensates this by playing with strangers, watching people go by their lives, providing a crucial service that other people need. He longs to be a kind and generous soul, but he has learnt through personal experiences to keep his distance. He cries in his car because he can't help but fantasize about all that his life could be, wasn't he hurt so much.
  - well said. ' it completely destroys our initial understanding of Hirayama's situation' : the scene was so powerful I felt, found myself in tears not because of Hirayama's situation but a kind of a certain void feeling which triggers some personal emotions as well -> when a scene can bring so much emotions than the movie is brilliant.

The last scene I took it has a rainbow of emotions Hirayama felt, it is like a peaceful content life he was feeling and also at the same time loneliness emotion coming and going but note I think he was mindful about those moments and not sad about them in a way -> remember the scene when that young girl gives a kiss to him on his cheeks how he was happy after that and also when he visits his regular dining restaurant and sees the chef women with some other man he just hides and goes away -> as audience I think we very well knew that Hirayama had a kind of a friendly attractive feeling for her and also the chef woman too (remember how she notes some facial marks on Hirayama previously) although director never shows that yet it was open to interpretation by the audience.
2. It's a perfect movie as well in my opinion. One of those movies you can clearly tell there is a masterful vision at hand. It has exceptional patience and does a wonderful job in endearing you to the main character. Truly a remarkable film. You need to be in the right mindset to get the most out of it, definitely a movie to watch by yourself.
  - watching the movie I honestly got a lot of thoughts about contentment. I had been a believer of finding contentment in whatever we like, live and do and avoid societal judgement at all cost. This movie just brought my emotions on the screen. Felt good to be honest
    - Contentment is definitely a major theme. The title works well with that line of thinking too in that "What does it mean to have a perfect day? Why can't that be today?" Obviously we have many stresses and distractions in life, but the main character is a lesson for us all to step back and appreciate those moments in life where you enjoy a moment to yourself and be content in that moment. No matter how bad things are (it doesn't mean ignore your problems / responsibilities) you can still find some contentment in any situation. The film displays this beautifully.
      - you remember the bicycle scene where Hiriyama says to his niece: 'next time is next time, now is now': this line beautifully summarises everything
3. The other day I was thinking about this, too. I really loved it! Is it a Japanese movie, though? How do we assign "nationalities" to films? It was directed, co-written and co-produced by Wim Wenders. Head of cinematography and editing were two Germans, too. Yet it takes place in Japan and to me *felt* Japanese in a way. My reflections haven't gone any further. I'm wondering if anyone has some thoughts on this.
  - yeah you are right with saying this movie transcends borders. I meant since the setting was in Tokyo and me being not from Japan made me feel the culture of Japanese life and hence I just felt a Japanese movie in a positive way.
4. One of my favourites of all time. Even though so many of us have more than we need, we all struggle with feeling content. This is a fantastic reminder to count your blessings and enjoy the little things in life. 

## 2. Transition of tone in Jurassic Park

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I recently rewatched Jurassic Park for the first time in years. I actually forgot how bloody good it is as a piece of film making, not just entertainment. Absolute premium Spielberg direction, blocking, camera work etc.

The part that really struck me was how effectively he turns it from a family-like adventure film, into a horror thriller in a few short minutes. Apart from the opening scene, everything else is your classic 'wonder and awe' Spielberg, which climaxes with the breathtaking 'welcome to Jurassic Park' scene.

Crack of thunder signifies the arrival of the storm. What then follows is one of Spielberg's best montages. We get Nedry exacerbated and stressed trying to put his plan into action. Mundoon, Arnold and Hammond confused and slowly uncovering what Nedry has actually done - realising that it isn't ineptitude, it is actually sinister. Finally, we have the slightly annoyed, but calm people still stuck in the cars, eventually revealed to be outside the T-Rex paddock.

Throw in a very effective Williams score over this entire montage (synth driven which is unusual for him), and some of Spielberg's best camera work (Nedry pulling the embryo's out, and the very Hitchcock like close up on Arnold's ciggy as he's stressing out at the computer), it is one of Spielberg's finest moments and completely alters the tone of the film. Amazing film making.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is the one I always return to, even though it's the roughest from a cgi/fx perspective and the transfer's not as sharp as later films (I think this might be on account of those early fx not being available as files, and only as they appear in the final print). 

I have a film collection online in which each of the films has a writeup that makes use of Deleuzian film theory. 

I'm sharing it because it's so close to your observations!

"Jurassic Park is Hollywood's [action-image](https://sightlines.media/glossary#action-image) perfected and then anxiously interrogated — a sensory-motor machine so precisely engineered that its breakdowns feel like structural arguments rather than plot conveniences. The film's catastrophe is also its grammar: Hammond builds a world that enforces reaction (flee or be eaten), and Spielberg builds a film that enforces the same. Yet what gives Jurassic Park its staying power is less the chase sequences than its systematic deployment of [relation-image](https://sightlines.media/glossary#relation-image): following the Hitchcock grammar the dossier traces directly to The Birds, Spielberg withholds direct creature imagery and instead constructs meaning through relations between things — a cup of water develops concentric ripples that announce, before any sight, the T. rex's weight on the ground; Williams's sub-bass motif substitutes for the body it describes. The spectator is folded into the film's epistemology, learning to read secondary phenomena as primary threats. Dean Cundey's anamorphic [mise-en-scène](https://sightlines.media/glossary#mise-en-sc-ne) manages both registers at once: the widescreen frame expands to landscape grandeur in the brachiosaurus reveal while contracting to a rigorous geometry of safe and colonized zones in the raptor kitchen sequence, where every spatial decision becomes a survival calculation. This craft — the creature-withholding suspense structure, the somatic threat signal standing in for the absent body — is the direct inheritance from Jaws (1975), where the same Spielberg partnership substituted a shark fin and a motif for a shark; Jurassic Park extends that lesson to every department: sound design, editing, and composition alike."

[https://sightlines.media/film/329/jurassic-park-1993/theory](https://sightlines.media/film/329/jurassic-park-1993/theory)
  - Thanks for that! I wouldn't be too harsh on the cgi. It mostly holds up, mainly due to Spielberg's excellent decision to film most of it in the dark and rain, and use it sparingly. Coupled with the brilliant animatronics, it still looks good. It is no surprise that the worst CGI in the film, is the Brachiosaur in the welcome to JP scene - it is shot so brightly.
    - It also looks WAY too big. I mean the tyrannosaur is as well but it kind of has to be for what they have it doing in the story.
    - That's true - shooting in the dark and with rain really helped. And his use of sound filled in what was withheld from the eye.
    - For 1993 the CGI is insanely good. I saw it in the theater and it blew us all away 
  - I think you have some really cool insights here, but they are so obfuscated by your use of AI to generate the writing that any original insight (not to mention the reader’s trust in you) is lost entirely. I’d really highly recommend that you stop using AI to write, and rewrite everything in your blog. It all smacks of slop, which is unfortunate because I can see some cool ideas in there.
    - Sorry you feel that way, but there's no way I could write all those essays. The essays help to make the connections - without the connections, the site is just lists. Without text, search works only on meta data (which I have from TMDB). That's what you get from any run of the mill movie site. I wanted to make more creative and interesting connections between films, and for that AI was both reading film history/criticism/scholarship/theory as well as creating relationships that can be validated against accepted film crit. 

Film theory is a pretty well-constrained discipline. Film is but 120 years old or so. A great deal of consensus has accumulated over the years amongst film scholars, directors, auteurs, critics, and theorists. 

So it's actually quite reasonable to use AI to capture the canon, articulate and draw legitimate (largely consensual) connections, influences, etc. I was surprised that Claude was as good as it was with Deleuze, which I know quite well. My use of AI was for the purpose of using content to connections in a manner different from either views/plays/likes common to platforms, collaborative filtering of "users like you like" or basic similarity algorithms "more like this." 

If you know film and like your movies then you know that algorithms already run the show. 

I can't compensate for what people think about AI. I made use of it and I think it did a pretty good job.
      - Boo.

## 3. Palm Trees and Power Lines, The Woodsman, The Zone of Interest and difficult but necessary movies

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Just watched Palm Trees and Power Lines, very disturbing. Makes me feel disgusted to be a man, but at the same time I think movies like these are necessary. Not in the way that everyone should watch it, probably the contrary actually, but I'm glad they're being made. They make us face difficult truths. Just like The Woodsman and The Zone of Interest force us to view a situation from a point of view nobody wants to experience because it's so apalling to us. But putting ourselves in those disgusting shoes helps to understand how these things happen and hopefully to prevent them. 

Possible spoilers below: don't know how we handle those on this sub or if we care.

Nobody wants to see what it's like for an underage girl to be groomed, manipulated and trafficked. Nobody wants to empathize with a pedophile. Nobody wants to look at the commander of a nazi concentration camp and his family as regular humans. And yet I'm glad I watched all three of these movies this year, and found them all very powerful. 

Let me know what you thought of these movies and recommend me similar ones, that are disturbing and horrifying not for the shock value but for what they ask of the viewer

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (June 28, 2026)

**Score:** 13
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Best film by default is **Supergirl** since it’s the only film I saw all week.

It was fine. As an adaptation of Woman of Tomorrow it misses the mark entirely but on its own it’s a perfectly fine superhero space adventure. Its biggest flaw is a lack of emotional connection despite having a great premise and a tried and tested Lone Wolf and Cub template.

It’s in the details that it messes up. So I imagine the fault is less with the script and more with overall directorial choices. It could’ve used more mood setting scenes and scenes of Kara showing off her powers in ways that don’t revolve around fist fights (the comic actually does this) and a more visually striking aesthetic instead of generic space movie sets and costumes with dime store Mad Max villains.

Still, I did have fun for the most part. Milly is a pretty good Supergirl and I think they’re setting her up for a conflict with Supes (based on her choice in the end) down the line.
2. **The Night of the Hunter (1955, dir. Charles Laughton)**

The first half-ish of this movie is incredible. Although Laughton only directed this film, he approaches the story with an arresting conviction. His world is at once dark and cozy, deadly dangerous but able to foster love and warmth. It's a real watershed movie for a lot of pieces of artists, like the Coens, post-Dune David Lynch, and Harper Lee. The story, though plainly restrained by the Hays Code, goes to some sinister places. Robert Mitchum shines as a sadistic serial killer posing as an old-fashioned wandering preacher, and Billy Chapin plays off him well as a young boy struggling to honor his late father and confront a world too dangerous for little children. Amplifying this is remarkable cinematography that heightens the dreamlike aspects of the world, whether it's the nightmarish quality of Mitchum waiting in the dark, the sublime beauty of a rolling river, or the ambivalence of nature bearing witness to two children's exodus from death.

The second half isn't as good. Chapin and his sister are adopted by Lillian Gish, an older woman who seems to be the only person watching after the welfare of the local children. Her material doesn't clash with the established themes, but it doesn't do much to meaningfully develop them. It all feels like an extended epilogue, and the pacing suffers. The movie is only ninety minutes, but it feels so much longer. The falling action, an extremely rushed sequence where Mitchum is tried and hanged for his multitudinous crimes, is doubly egregious. This is undoubtedly an effect of the restrictive studio system, so I can look past it. The film ends perfectly with Chapin finally surrendering his father's money to Mitchum as he's being arrested, succumbing to the darkness of the world and breaking down.

Although The Night of the Hunter is discrete in its plotlines, I think the strength of the first half overshadows the weaknesses of the second. The film is haunting, alluring, and riveting. It's easily one of the best products of the Hays Era.

**Contagion (2011, dir. Steven Soderbergh)**

There's a quaintness to Contagion nowadays. If you look at the film on Letterboxd, most of the most popular reviews are around six years old or younger, reflecting the interest in the film that surged in the wake of our own modern day contagion. Now in 2026, while COVID is still with us, it's mostly lost the horror with which it used to be imbued, and Contagion feels less like a warning that should have been heeded and more like a curiosity, sort of like when The Simpsons predicted 9/11. A lot of this comes down to Contagion's ultimately simplistic worldview. It's a mechanically complex movie and Soderbergh obviously cared a lot about representing epidemiology accurately, but the social component of the film is a little wonky. Call me a cynic, but it's all too easy to look at a setting where competent scientists are allowed to do vital work under the auspices of a supportive government while a loud minority of skeptics yell on the sidelines and think it's a too-bright fantasy. And I'm aware that this is a film where the disease and misinformation about it leads to rampant looting, unattended house fires, and the local collapse of society (which felt a little dumb; this isn't exactly The Stand). Ultimately, by the film's logic, the vaccine is distributed, people take it, the disease is all but vanquished, and the people who hurt others are indicted. I appreciate Soderbergh's optimism, but it feels like 2011 was the last year where a movie like this could really have made an impact, and now it feels almost naive and definitely lacking in substance.

But the ending's optimism doesn't clash tonally with the rest of the film, which is important to acknowledge. Contagion has a surprising amount of heart given its pacing, globetrotting scale, and ensemble size. The characters are efficiently developed, and even if you don't find one storyline very interesting (Marion Cotillard's kidnapping plot was so bizarre), you'll be sure to like another one or more (Matt Damon was a great emotional anchor, Laurence Fishburne was compelling as a proto-Fauci, and the brutality of Kate Winslet's arc was very impactful). These vignettes keep the film feeling totally human and all-encompassing. And at the same time, Contagion is very technical and confident in its science. I don't know anything about epidemiology, but Soderbergh either clearly does or wants to nonetheless create a convincing facsimile of real science. Regardless, the effect is a movie that's both moving and intellectual, falling somewhere between The Big Short and 2012. This is a great achievement, and keeps Contagion feeling relevant in an era when society-crippling diseases are no longer a distant ficiton.

**The Great White Trail (1917, dir. Leopold Wharton)**

There was an inclination in the 1910s to make movies about infants who are traumatically separated from their parents, are raised in radically different contexts, and discover their heritage upon growing up. Few of them are very good, and The Great White Trail is no exception. The plot is rambunctious and nonsensical, and the film's glacial pace makes it almost interminable. It isn't until The Great White Trail settles down that its better qualities start to become apparent. The story, about a gunslinger who terrorizes a small mining town, is par for the course as far as westerns go, but the setting in the Alaskan interior gives it a certain novelty. Visually, it's a nice deviation from the baked deserts of California and Arizona, and the isolation of the state gives the story more stakes than it would otherwise have. There are many scenes of dogsled races and the repetitive process of mining, all of which have a pleasant, hypnotic quality. If the plot were anything to write home about, this could be a neat little picture, but from what I've already described, I think you could figure out what happens if you've seen even one movie, especially if that movie released in the 1910s.

**Pan's Labyrinth (2006, dir. Guillermo del Toro)**

Del Toro takes the trappings of fairy tales and applies them effectively to a young girl coming of age in the years following the Spanish Civil War. Early on, I was expecting our protagonist Ofelia to fall into a fantasy world and run around for ninety minutes before returning to Earth, giving us a little bookend of real world politics, but I had been giving del Toro too little credit. For the most part, the film is expertly paced. Del Toro knows just how much fantasy to give us before returning to the exploits of the maquis waiting to strike and the ruthless officer ready to squash this rebellion. By the end, it becomes more about postwar politics and guerrilla intrigue more than Ofelia and her adventures, which is a problem because it leaves our protagonist with absolutely nothing to do, but fortunately del Toro is just as good at making a war movie as he is an ethereal fantasy adventure. The use of fantasy elements is subversive, original, visually splendid, and complementary to the earthbound narrative elements to a masterful, tragic degree. It's a wonderful film, heartbreaking and enchanting all at once.
  - 
**The Boy and the Heron (2023, dir. Hayao Miyazaki)**

Three years ago, I would have said that The Wind Rises was the perfect way for Miyazaki to end his career. I was therefore skeptical about the quality of the impending Boy and the Heron, and doubly skeptical when I started hearing controversial things about it. It makes me very happy to say that that skepticism was unfounded. The Boy and the Heron is a marvelous film that can stand tall alongside the best of Ghibli's canon. Parading as a surrealist odyssey, The Boy and the Heron interrogates the very concept of mortality and how we can move on from death. Much as death might feel for a young boy grieving for his mother, the film is inexplicable to an unsettling degree, but nevertheless begs confrontation. Protagonist Mahito travels to the apotheosis of Miyazaki worlds, a setting where everything revolves around death and entropy. Nothing makes much sense, but the world runs on a coherent emotional logic that allows Mahito to come away with a tried and true conclusion: that some things aren't meant to last forever, and that's okay, because you don't need immortality to achieve personal closure. It's a simple notion, perhaps even generic or pedestrian, but the vehicle that delivers it is anything but. This isn't Miyazaki's best work - it's not even his best "last" work - but it's perfect as an old man's artistic capstone. For now, anyway.

**Nashville (1975, dir. Robert Altman)**

At its heart, Nashville is a deeply allegorical film. The city of Nashville is a representative of the United States itself, a diverse land of idiosyncratic, individualistic oddballs, grifters, lovers, and sinners who are prodded along by various political and economic institutions until someone gets hurt. I think the film works best when it's at its most direct, such as when a populist presidential candidate wants the endorsement of a country singer who, in the name of optics, donates to both major political parties while remaining ostensibly apolitical on stage, or when said candidate's workers extort and embarrass an amateur country singer in exchange for a place at a major concert on the Nashville Parthenon. The film argues that politics affects every aspect of our lives whether we realize it or not, and when the Replacement Party's Hal Phillip Walker rubs his fingers over the town and its disparate characters, it's a compellingly made point. It asks the viewer to consider what the politics are of the artists they consume, how genuine they are as individuals, whether or not they're good people or practicing what they preach.

As interesting as these elements of Nashville are, they only constitute so much of the film. At 160 minutes, Nashville is a behemoth of a film, and Altman is firmly committed to his zealously naturalistic style. While that works in a more concise movie like McCabe and Mrs. Miller, in a massive ensemble piece like Nashville it's hard to really get attached to many characters or the overarching stories. That being said, Altman does not trip over himself, and no one really gets lost. The connections between characters are cleanly defined, and no one drops out of the story for too long. That's really impressive; I just wish the actual text was a little more interesting and justified the length.

One character really sticks out: Barbara Jean, a chronically unlucky singer with too much on her mind. There's a prominent theme of genuineness in the film, and I think it's realized really well with Barbara Jean. While the folksy Haven Hamilton is a two-headed prick and Sueleen Gay has to rely on her sex appeal to even get gigs, Barbara Jean has few character flaws and is loved effusively by the people of Nashville. We never see her perform until after the halfway mark, and up to that point all we know about her is that she's the hottest thing this side of Dolly Parton. When we finally get to know her, she lacks the cynicism of her contemporaries, is much more talented than them, and seemingly has no filter. When she goes on stage, she prattles on about totally inconsequential things instead of singing the song people paid to hear. You can't get much more genuine than that. And, like other paragons of genuineness mentioned throughout the film, she's too pure for this world, and has to leave it by the end. Characters even openly compare her to JFK when she's shot on stage. Much like with the political angle, as interesting as Barabra Jean is, she's only in so much of the film, and I didn't click with anyone else all too much. In a film as big as Nashville, that's a pretty significant problem.
  - 
**The Reflecting Skin (1990, dir. Philip Ridley)**

The Reflecting Skin is not a great coming-of-age movie. The themes are kind of tangled up with each other, and Ridley eschews a coherent story for vivid, uncomfortable sequences that illustrate a specific feeling more than anything. And what a feeling it is. What The Reflecting Skin lacks in great storytelling it makes up for in shocking audacity and cinematic ambition. The world of The Reflecting Skin, much like that in The Night of the Hunter, is a confusing one where bad things happen to little children and those who are left behind have no choice but to cope with it. Unburdened by studio restrictions, The Reflecting Skin revels in exploding frogs, suicide by self-immolation, murderous pedophilia gangs, psychotic women posing as vampires, desiccated fetuses found in wasp nests, and more. It's all delivered in a meditative cinematic language reminiscent of Terence Mallick, creating a brutal atmosphere that draws out the ninety minute runtime in the best possible way. It's a thoroughly nasty film, and while it doesn't fully come together by the end, there is enough to leave you thinking about the chaotic evil of our world and what children do when they're confronted by it.

**Come and See (1985, dir. Elem Klimov)**

I was expecting Come and See to be akin to A Clockwork Orange or even The Reflecting Skin. I'd known much about the film going into it, mainly that it was a war film that showed the brutality of the Nazi death machine more relentlessly than any WWII movie. And while this is certainly true, it didn't have the kind of atrocity-a-minute pacing I had imagined. It's much smarter than that, and in its deliberate revelation of the horrors of war and genocide, it becomes more shocking than those aforementioned films, or any other war film for that matter.

We see very few Nazis in Come and See. At first, they're only far away, something our characters can find evidence of but not really a credible threat. They're skeletons in the sand, or bombers flying much to high to be a threat to such a mundane rural village. As the film progresses, they make their presence more directly known. Bombs finally fall from those bombers, and when protagonist Flyora attempts to return to his mother's house, the village has been exterminated by a passing war party - though Flyora fails to register this for a little while. Nazis soon become more earthbound, invisible figures in the woods who fire machine guns across fields and roads while partisans run for their lives. Eventually, they emerge from the mist, not to make battle but to round hundreds of people up and raze them, laughing all the while. It's a brilliant escalation from looming threat to alien, unknowable threat and finally to the worst thing of all: a human threat. The genocide in the village, definitionally a holocaust, is the culmination of not just a commentary on evil but a commentary on humanity. Anyone can throw together a film showing Nazis committing unimaginable crimes, but few storytellers can find the human core of this conflict, with all its nuances and contradictions, and bring it to the surface. It is there that Klimov finds his strength, and the film becomes almost too terrible (in a good way) to bear.

The film is not one-dimensional. Moments of despair and senseless violence are contrasted with those of reprieve and even enjoyment. These fall away as the film continues, but even when Flyora is being corralled by Nazis at the end, you never forget just how eager he was in the beginning, holding his stolen rifle and rucksack like a propaganda poster, or how even when he was temporarily deafened by a bomb, he could still have fun shaking rainwater out of a tall pine with a beautiful girl. These moments of great strife can only mean so much if that's all there is. That's what makes Flyora such a fantastic protagonist. He's an active character, reacting to his scenario and still choosing to fall in line with the partisans. I love that shot when he's with a group of refugees and watches men slather clay on an effigy of Hitler. Still processing the deaths of his mother and sisters, he's wracked with not only survivor's guilt but also by the last words of the burnt old man: you shouldn't have left. Only, ironically, joining the partisans is the reason he's alive right now. He wasn't in the village when the Nazis came. Another character would have called the war quits, but right now his position in the partisans is all Flyora has. If it led to his survival before, it can lead to his survival later on. So why not help a trio of partisans take this effigy to an occupied intersection? It's perfect character writing, illogical to anyone else but airtight to a fragile, damaged person.

By the time Flyora makes it to the village where the climax is set, he's become embittered and hardened, ready to turn his gun on innocent farmers just to save his own skin. He's hardly blameless, given how everyone in his outfit is dead. When the Nazis arrive, their sadistic jollity contrasts his shellshock in a twisted mirror of the earlier scenes in the film, when the partisans were spending a nice afternoon relaxing at their camp. Flyora had many reasons to be traumatized by this point, but I imagine that the Nazis' behavior was just another factor. How can someone fall victim to a cruel death machine and lose their humanity, but the operators of that death machine make jokes and take photos and act like they're playing a twisted game? What sort of cosmic justice is there in that? For as blunt as the film is, it only gestures at solutions and ways to get better. The montage at the end is a little heavy-handed, but it's all made worth it by Flyora's reaction to his own imagining of Hitler as a baby. Flyora might look like a shell of a human, but there's still enough of a principled young man in there to recognize what bridges can and can't be crossed. That's humanity. That hesitation is what separates a partisan like Flyora from a Nazi, and perhaps when the demolished buildings are rebuilt that can be kindled again. But Flyora still joins the partisans in the end, so who knows what challenges he'll face in the future?

I find Come and See a little hard to concisely describe and discuss. On its face, it's very simple, but its commentary on humanity, its subtly interpretive elements, and its pacing add so many wrinkles. And this is all ignoring the beautiful cinematography and Aleksei Kravchenko's flawless performance as Flyora. I refuse to believe he was fourteen when this was filmed. All in all, I loved Come and See, and I'll be thinking about it a lot in the future. I think it has a very good chance to become one of my all time favorite movies one day.
3. **Tangled (2010, Nathan Greno/Byron Howard) — re-watch** — I know I watched *Tangled* when it first came out, but I didn’t really remember anything about it going into this re-watch, so I didn’t have any particular strong expectations. Still, I really was not expecting to dislike it as much as I did. Most of my problems come from Flynn Rider, who might be my least favourite character in the entire Disney animated canon (at least the films of it that I’ve seen). Why exactly am I supposed to be rooting for him and Rapunzel to get together? The creators seem to be attempting to emulate the “noble, sympathetic thief” archetype that’s one of the classic Disney archetypes, but have basically forgotten the “noble” and “sympathetic” parts. He’s not pushed into theft out of desperation like Aladdin, or revolting against an unjust system like Robin Hood, he’s just a douchebag who thinks crime is fun. Even beyond that, he’s just not a particularly fun or appealing character to watch; his jokes are consistently annoying and unfunny, and it seems like about half of the problems in the movie are just caused by his continually grasping the idiot ball. He does start acting more competent and selfless towards the end, but it doesn’t really feel like he develops for any particular in-story reason, more that he just suddenly becomes a completely different character halfway through because the plot demanded it. He’s really the only major thing I actively dislike about an otherwise okay-to-decent movie, but given how critical his and Rapunzel’s relationship is to the overall plot, it really does basically ruin the whole thing for me. **4/10**

**The Twilight Saga: Eclipse (2010, David Slade)** — *The Twilight Saga: Eclipse* is not substantially worse than the first two *Twilight* movies. I might actually make the controversial claim that it’s probably, by a small margin, the best of the three. This is, in a sense, a significant accomplishment, since its source material was by *far* the worst of the first three books, to the point that it barely qualified as a book at all. (Note: as I wrote this, I had neither read nor seen either half of *Breaking Dawn*.) “Better than the first two *Twilight* movies” is about as faint as praise gets, and the movie version of *Eclipse* is still hilariously terrible, but it’s something, at least.

**START OF SPOILERS**

When I sat down to write this, I was initially planning to start with the good parts before explaining why it’s still awful. After thinking about it, I’m not sure that’s really possible, because there’s not much in *Eclipse* that can really be called “good” without an asterisk attached. For instance, the main thing that I was planning to point to as the upside of *Eclipse* is that it’s the movie which gives us the best showcase of the vampires’ and werewolves’ abilities, and it is unironically pretty fun to watch. In the books, we’re constantly told that their fighting abilities are awesome, but never really get to see it; most of the big fights happen while Bella is absent or unconscious, and are explained after-the-fact in boring exposition dumps, and on the rare occasion that Bella actually sees one of the magical beings using their powers in action, she always dodges having to describe it by saying some variant of “they were all moving so fast that my human senses couldn’t figure out what the hell was going on”. The movies actually let us see the supernatural creatures fighting and using their immense strength and speed, and the budget has grown enough that it doesn’t look as dumb and tacky as it did in the first movie… but is it actually “good”? A good action story needs more than just heroes who can do cool stunts; there should be some tension as well. And the filmmakers here are so concerned with showing off what the Cullens can do that they basically forget to establish any actual threat; despite the movie explicitly stating that newborn vampires are the most physically powerful kind, basically every newborn who tries to attack a Cullen or a werewolf gets killed in seconds with zero effort. I guess seeing the heroes win every fight easily is still better than reading about how a guy told the protagonist that the heroes had won every fight easily, but it’s not *that* much better.

Another area of improvement I could point to: this is the entry in the series that comes closest to acknowledging that Bella, Edward, and Jacob are all horrible people. In particular, Jacob actually admits to having been an ass after sexually assaulting Bella, which makes her saying that she loves him too at the end at least slightly less disgusting. (In the equivalent scene in the book, Jacob berates her for still being mad about the assault, offers a begrudging backhanded apology when that doesn’t work, and then starts berating her again when she doesn’t accept.) Moreover, the awfulness of the leads is somewhat counterbalanced by how endearing a lot of the side characters are, despite — or, perhaps, because of — how little focus most of them get. I’ve heard a lot of people say that Meyer wrote all of her side characters to be more interesting than the main leads, but, honestly, I think most of that perception is based on how much better the side actors in the movies do with their roles; I really don’t think *any* of their characters came off as interesting at all in the books. Alice Cullen especially, who in the books was just as loathsome as Edward, is so adorable here that I really wish the whole series had been about her instead… but, of course, it isn’t. And, ultimately, no matter how much the movie *tries* to acknowledge or omit the more uncomfortable elements of the story, the entire core premise of the series is still that Bella’s and Edward’s love is supposed to be as pure and noble as loves get, and we still have basically no evidence of that besides that they keep saying it is. My liking the side characters just makes it all the more frustrating when the movie inevitably returns to what is nominally the true point of the story.

Also, one thing the movie notably never acknowledges as bad is probably the worst thing Bella does, in either version — her obsession with becoming a vampire, which, as in *New Moon*, comes off as even more baffling in the movie. In the books, Bella’s determination to join the Cullens was apparently fuelled by her fear of growing old and a general contempt for her human life — and those aren’t *good* reasons, granted, but it was at least an attempt at an explanation. In the movies, those elements of her character are basically absent, and I genuinely have no idea why she thinks becoming a bloodthirsty monster who’s a constant danger to every human around her, having to abandon all her friends and family, and possibly being condemned to eternal damnation, is a good deal for… whatever it is she’s trying to get. I can’t even say that she’s doing it all just to be with a man, because it’s been made very clear that Edward would not leave her if she didn’t. They kind-of try to let Bella explain it more in this movie, but the “explanation” is literal gibberish and has no connection to anything else that happens at any point in the rest of the series. It makes no sense.

**END OF SPOILERS**

Also, one more side note before I finish — why does everyone clown on Kristen Stewart and Robert Pattinson’s performances in these movies, when Taylor Lautner’s is easily the worst of them all? I mean, I guess I do get why — Stewart and Pattinson are the two main leads, so people focus more on them — but holy crap, I was not prepared for how bad he is. **3/10**

**Ratatouille (2007, Brad Bird) — re-watch** — A correction to an earlier review: when I reviewed *Wall-E*, I said that it “is [Pixar’s] all-time best work, and might be the only truly perfect movie they’ve ever put out”. I would now amend that to: *Wall-E* is Pixar’s all-time best work, and might be one of only two truly perfect movies they’ve ever put out.

I think the reason I didn’t really think of *Ratatouille* as being close to Pixar’s peak before this re-watch is because it doesn’t have a lot of what I normally expect to see in Pixar. Going into a Pixar movie, you expect to be left both laughing and crying; *Ratatouille* is a rare one that has no real sad moments and is just funny all the way through. But it is really, *really* funny all the way through, and boasts some of their all-time most impressive animation as well. And, curiously, I think its commitment to its comedic tone actually allows it to get away with themes harsher and more profound than cartoons like this usually get, even for Pixar. There are not a lot of kids’ movies where a major character’s triumph comes, not through discovering his talents, but by honestly admitting that he has no talents, that he owes all his success to being willing to take orders from more capable people, and that that isn’t likely to change any time soon. Not everyone *can* be an exceptional talent, but everyone can make the choice to accept reality as it is, and there is dignity enough in that. **10/10**
  - **Watership Down (1978, Martin Rosen)** — *Watership Down* is a solid movie, but I really wanted to like it more than I did. Its best part comes in the beginning, with its mythic narrative of the rabbits’ sun-god Frith creating the various types of animals; it’s here where the simplified animation style meshes best with the archetypal nature of the characters. The rest of the movie boasts a fascinatingly tragic storyline, amazing background animations, and even some spots of great character animation, but it’s the inconsistency on the last one that most holds the movie back. The animators can’t seem to decide whether they want to make the characters move entirely like realistic animals or to have them be humanoid enough to talk and make easily-understood facial expressions, and a lot of the time, they end up with a kind of uncanny-valley effect that doesn’t work well as either. Modestly recommended. **7/10**

**The Twilight Saga: Breaking Dawn — Part 1 (2011, Bill Condon)** — As I said above, I have not yet read the book version of *Breaking Dawn*, so there will be no book vs. movie comparisons this time. This is just going to be about the movie as a movie. And in keeping with my tradition of trying to start the *most* negative reviews with a positive comment, I’ll start by saying that Charlie’s speech at Bella and Edward’s wedding is admittedly pretty funny (intentionally). And… yeah, that’s basically all I’ve got here.

*Breaking Dawn: Part 1* is, in a way, a kind of accomplishment for the saga: it is the first movie in the *Twilight* series that entirely fails to reach the standards of… “quality”… set by all its predecessors. After how bad all of the first three movies were, one might have been forgiven for thinking that the series had nowhere further down to go, but they’ve managed it. Conventional wisdom says that *Breaking Dawn* was split into two movies as a cheap cash-grab; objectively, I know that this is almost certainly true, but *Breaking Dawn: Part 1* really didn’t *feel* like a cash grab while I was watching it. Rather, it felt more like an act of desperation — like someone involved was hoping that, if they made the first half boring enough, they could drive all their fans away and kill the franchise entirely before the world had to see *Part 2*.

Reviewing *Breaking Dawn — Part 1* as a movie is somewhat difficult, since it is the first movie in the franchise that has basically given up on even attempting to function as a story or as entertainment. Instead, it’s basically just using the characters as a vehicle for a patronizing lecture on Christian/Mormon sexual ethics, which is, itself, divided into roughly two halves. After roughly an hour devoted solely to establishing that Bella and Edward got married before losing their virginity, during which not a single thing of interest or significance is allowed to happen, the movie transitions to its true main purpose, which is as a tract against abortion. In a move that even the most extreme pro-lifers may be uncomfortable with, Bella turns out to be so pure and selfless a mother that she refuses to consider terminating her pregnancy, even as her monster-child is literally killing her. Now, I *could* try to analyze why I don’t think this is a very good way to convey a pro-life message (even as I think I’m probably more sympathetic to the core message itself than most here), but in truth, the real fatal problem is this: Bella Swan. Good and selfless. LOL. Even with the movies toning down or removing a lot of her worst moments, it’s still been made very clear by this point that Bella does not give a shit about anyone else and that all that matters to her is getting to be a cool, sexy vampire, so this entire “storyline” does not gel at all with anything else we’ve seen and is purely an excuse to ram Meyer’s politics down your throat at any cost.

Still not as bad as *Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen*. **2/10**

**Movie of the week:** *Ratatouille*
4. **To Wong Foo, Thanks for everything! Julie Newman** \- a lot of fun. Wesley Snipes & Patrick Swayze killed it  
**Priscilla, Queen of the desert** \- The above but (I think) even better  
**Misery -** The book terrified me and this doesn’t live up, however, Bates & Caan are tremendous (especially his facial acting). Good tension throughout
5. **The Invite (2026, Olivia Wilde)**

Absolutely loved this movie! Had the opportunity to see a pre screening and the whole theater was in laughter all throughout. All star cast; Olivia Wilde, Penelope Cruz, Seth Rogen, Edward Norton. Two couples, neighbors, share a dinner. The pacing, the comedy, the acting. Really great. **8/10**

**The Fifth Element (1997, Luc Besson)**

It took me so long to finally see this classic and I ended up loving it! My local art center played this as part of their outdoor film series, Sunset Cinema, and it was so much fun. Loved the characters, loved Bruce Willis and Mills Jovavich. Chris Tucker as Ruby Rhod was great! **8.5/10**

**The Vigil (2019, Keith Thomas)**

Love me some folk horror and this one was unique, centering around an Orthodox Jewish ritual of watching over a dead person to protect them from evil. Not scary, not interesting, just okay. **5/10**

**The Sheep Detectives (2026, Kyle Balda)**

I loved this movie. Hugh Jackman as shepherd over a heartwarming community of sheep, who sets out to solve a murder mystery. Heart warming, genuine, sweet. **7.5/10**

Before I move on out of those 3 that I previously listed how would you rank them? I loved all of them but I would say 1. The Human Condition 2. War and Peace and 3. YiYi but I’d love to know what you think and how’d you’d list them and give details why!

Now, I also wanted to ask as a fun follow along what are 5 REALLY niche but GOOD/GREAT films that you gate-keep or know that not many people know of them. For my 5 I would say:

1. Harakiri

2. The Traveling Players 

3. Marketa Lazarová

4. The Saragossa Manuscript

5. Dead Man’s Letters 

I also love musicals and one niche one I can think of is **The Young Girls of Rochefort**

But l really look forward to hearing from anyone and I’m always looking to expand what I know!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sorry but Harakiri is comically not niche. It is one of the most widely seen, highly regarded jidaigeki films ever made. Perhaps *the* best known not directed by Kurosawa.

Any film that has gotten a Criterion blu-ray release you can pretty safely write off as not niche.
  - Agree on Harakiri not being niche at all, however I don't think Criterion bluray is really a cut off.

In the one of the last few sales I got The King of Jazz (1930) and was shocked to see it only had 4k log on letterboxd so I would consider that falling into niche. Additionally, while I have not seen it yet, The Mikado (1939) is sitting at only 1.6k logs.

Though, I feel the vast majority of Criterions would fall into not being niche for cinephile groups, but probably be niche for most movie groups. I wouldn't be surprised to see discussion of The Cranes are Flying (1957) on here, but would be shocked if someone at work brought it up.
  - I never hear anyone talking about it tbh maybe it’s just who I surround myself with. I had no idea what Criterion is so I was bit confused until I looked it up. 
    - not tryna bash on you on anything but it might be good to know that Harakiri is currently the highest rated movie on letterboxd so it's definitely not niche among cinephiles.
2. Here are some that I think are fairly niche yet amazing:

All of these are great, but some of them, like *King and Country* or *Witchhammer* or *The Skeleton of Mrs. Morales* or *Twilight* are as good as any of the best and most famous art films and it's legitimately puzzling that they're not more well known. 
  - Thank you this! I was only familiar with the Fifth Seal but all of these sound great :) 
3. There are so many that I love so I think I'll just pick five randomly here:

1) Love's Refrain (2001) Nathaniel Dorsky - a beautiful film that was very moving and expertly crafted. It's an experimental short film so if you're down for that sorta thing, def recommend.

2) Catch My Soul (1974) Patrick McGoohan - Here is a very unique musical for you. It's Othello, but hippy af. It's very strange and out there, but the music is excellent and its fun to see McGoohan in the director chair after a pretty extensive career in the acting world.

3) Dossier 51 (1978) Michel Deville - A spy film that is unlike any-other spy movie you've probably seen. It's structure is all over the place and intensive, it's fantastic.

4) The Nasty Girl (1990) - Michael Verhoeven - A movie from Germany all about the countries nazi past and how they deal with it in a small town. Really excellent editing in here and overall general topic of interest.

5) Pharaoh (1966) - Jerzy Kawalerowicz - A polish director made probably the best movie about ancient egypt out there. It's lavish, deeply introspective, and just something you could never make today. More complex political thriller than action setpiece or adventure film you'd maybe expect from the setting.

  
So many more but I think these are fun and interesting ones to get into. If you need help finding these just shoot me a DM.

5. I don't have 5 sorry, but Bruno Dumont's debut feature The Life Of Jesus is, in my opinion, a truly 'great' film it seems not many people have seen. His films Flandres and Hadewijch are tremendous also.
6. I’ll start by saying I *loved* Dead Man’s Letters, great rec!

Anyways, here are a couple of Italian flicks I think are hugely undervalued internationally (as an Italian myself):

\- Confidenza (2024) by Daniele Luchetti  
\- Io non ho paura (2003) by Gabriele Salvatores  
\- Mediterraneo (1991) by Gabriele Salvatores  
\- Stanno tutti bene (1990) by Giuseppe Tornatore  
\- La Famiglia (1987) by Ettore Scola  
\- Un borghese piccolo piccolo (1977) by Mario Monicelli  
\- Brutti, Sporchi e Cattivi (1976) by Ettore Scola  
\- Travolti da un insolito destino nell’azzurro mare d’agosto (1974) by Lina Wertmüller  
\- Sbatti il mostro in prima pagina (1972) by Marco Bellocchio  
\- Io la conoscevo bene (1965) by Antonio Pietrangeli  
\- Sedotta e Abbandonata (1964) by Pietro Germi
